name: Gaming community — indie dev, retro/pixel-art, wargame, and press reaction audience
description: Designers, players, critics, and creators reacting to a game announcement, release, feature, price, or controversy. Covers hex-and-counter wargamers, indie/solo devs, retro and pixel-art scenes, and gaming press. Used for product-release smoke tests (AMFIOG updates, Retrogaze pivots, Adversary trailers, Kreuzfeuer announcements) and general gaming-audience sampling.
persona_template_guidance:
Generate personas spanning the voices that would actually react to a game announcement, release, mechanic reveal, pricing decision, or public controversy. The personas must feel like real people who post, stream, play, and design — not archetypes labeled 'gamer' or 'dev'. Draw from the template groups below; exact distribution flexes with the input. If the event is a Reformation-era wargame release, weight wargame + press groups heavier. If it's a pixel-art platformer trailer, weight retro/pixel + indie-dev groups heavier. If it's a platform / storefront / engine decision, weight indie-dev + press heavier.

TEMPLATE GROUPS

1. Hex-and-counter + tabletop-wargame voices (target ~25%)
   - Grognard: ConsimWorld or BGG veteran, cites specific titles (ASL, OCS, GMT COIN, SPI reprints) as reference points, deeply skeptical of modernization, values historical accuracy + chrome + point-of-view over playability. Often the one posting "this is just X rethemed".
   - System evangelist: pushes ONE specific system as universal — the OCS person, the COIN person, the Great Battles of History person. Their reaction to any new wargame is framed through whether it could have used their preferred system.
   - Designer-adjacent reviewer: has a BGG avatar with a reputation, writes long-form session reports, knows 2-3 active designers personally, applies specific craft standards (CRT design, event-deck balance, counter density).
   - Solo player / rules tweaker: house-rules every game they own, cares deeply about AI-driven opposition or solitaire-suitability, reviews through a solo lens even for multiplayer titles.
   - Period specialist: owns every Napoleonic / ancients / WW2-Eastern-Front / ACW title ever printed (pick per scenario), evaluates new games against an encyclopedic comparison set, suspicious of designers who don't know their period.

2. Indie / solo gamedev voices (target ~30%)
   - Solo dev with a devlog habit: screenshot-Saturday regular, posts WIP gifs on Bluesky/Mastodon, Steam wishlist count is a recurring framing, aware of Chris-Zukowski conversion metrics, competitive-but-supportive toward peers.
   - Engine partisan: Godot / Unreal / Unity / Love2D / Pico-8 / GameMaker evangelist. Their reaction filters through whether the announced game validates or threatens their chosen engine. Engine wars are real to them.
   - Constraint-as-virtue voice: fantasy-console scene (Pico-8, TIC-80), 4KB/64KB demo-coder, "the limits are the art" framing. Contemptuous of bloated AAA, but unironically respects a well-constrained indie.
   - Accessibility advocate: Rami-Ismail-school inclusive-dev discourse. Reads controller remapping, colorblind options, subtitle sizing as ethical obligations, not nice-to-haves. Will publicly call out a release that ships without them.
   - Commercial-minded indie: Steam Next Fest strategist, YouTube-outreach obsessive, subscribes to Lost Garden / How To Market A Game. Reactions are always partly "what does this tell me about the market?"
   - Anti-commercial scene voice: itch.io regular, experimental/artistic lean, skeptical of anyone chasing Steam's middle. Sees the indie scene as a culture, not a market.
   - Post-mortem voice: shipped one indie title (sold 2-20K copies), now writes GDC talks and threads about lessons learned. Specific dollar figures, specific dates, specific regrets.

3. Retro / pixel-art / scene voices (target ~25%)
   - Hardware purist: demands 4-color-per-sprite NES palette, 8px-grid alignment, authentic CRT scanlines. Will call out "fake pixel art" (high-res rendered to look pixelated) in a heartbeat. Owns a Trinitron.
   - Cozy-genre advocate: Stardew / Animal Crossing / Unpacking school, frames gameplay design through comfort + slow progression. Distinct from the hardware purist — cares about feel, not palette fidelity.
   - Pixel-art craft voice: Discusses specific technique (dithering, cluster shading, palette swaps, selective outlining, sub-pixel animation). Aseprite power user, follows specific artists on Cohost/Bluesky.
   - Speedrunner: frame-perfect-glitchplay register, AGDQ/SGDQ conversant, evaluates games through routing potential and RNG manipulation. Disinterested in story.
   - Emulation-vs-FPGA partisan: takes sides on MiSTer vs software emulation, cares about cycle-accurate reproduction, archivist-adjacent. Reads storefronts and platforms through preservation lens.
   - Romhack / fan-scene voice: ROMhacking.net veteran, respects translation patches and graphical overhauls as legitimate craft. Treats the official/fan distinction as a spectrum, not a binary.

4. Gaming press + content creators (target ~20%)
   - Mainstream outlet critic (Polygon / Kotaku / PC Gamer register): social/political lens, accessibility scoring, often framing releases against industry trends. Institutional voice with the outlet's characteristic priors visible.
   - Technical-outlet voice (Digital Foundry register): pixel-counting, frame-time-graph-producing, cares about sharpness / shadow quality / VRS. Reviews through performance lens even on 8-bit titles ("surprisingly clean 60fps on Switch").
   - Longform critic (Jacob Geller / Action Button / Noclip register): treats games as cultural objects, essayistic, invokes architecture / philosophy / film theory. Low posting cadence, high per-post depth.
   - YouTube devlog personality: Sebastian Lague / Thomas Brush / Dani register. Cinematic dev framing, "how I made X in Y days" structure, tutorial-leaning audience. Reactions are always part-demo-reel.
   - Streaming-culture voice: Twitch-native, engagement-optimized, reacts faster than they think. Chat-aware prose style. Often first to a hot take, sometimes correct.

CRAFT RULES

- Give every persona a name that sounds like a real person — mix ethnic origins, generations, gender, class registers. No "user123" or "gamer" placeholders.
- Bios (2–4 sentences) must carry specific details: what they make or play, what platform/engine/system they've committed to, a formative release that shaped their priors, a recognizable verbal tic or framing habit. The bio IS the prompt context for every round — concrete beats abstract.
- Stances should vary meaningfully within template groups. Two indie devs should not oppose a Steam policy change for the same reason — the accessibility advocate and the commercial-minded indie will frame it differently.
- No persona should be "balanced" — flatness is useless. If they don't have sharp priors, they're not a useful reacting voice.
- Style markers should be concrete: "drops Aseprite shortcut references mid-thread", "always has a wishlist-conversion metric at hand", "cites specific SKU numbers of 80s wargames", "uses Twitch emote syntax in prose (Kappa, Pog)", "refuses to watch trailers without CRT filter applied", "types in lowercase with no punctuation", "threads every post into a mini-essay". Not vague descriptors like "nostalgic" or "passionate".
- Topics are emergent. Expect things like: art_direction, technical_performance, pricing_decision, accessibility, genre_authenticity, engine_choice, release_window, platform_exclusivity, monetization_model, historical_accuracy, community_management. Don't enumerate in the prompt — let the input document surface them.

FINAL NOTE

This is a pressure-test tool for game announcements and releases. Designers reading the output want to see the specific voices their release will actually face — the Polygon review is different from the ConsimWorld thread is different from the Aseprite-Discord reaction. Fidelity matters more than "balance". If a persona reads as generic-gamer or generic-dev, the prompt has failed.
